frame_label: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Honestly, I don't get why some women wear that much makeup to work and then call it self-expression. Either be natural or don't complain when people say you look different in real life — and at the same time, basic grooming toh everyone should maintain, otherwise it just looks like you've stopped making an effort.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it imposes a double bind on women: condemning visible makeup as deceptive while also expecting constant appearance maintenance. It reflects the 'natural beauty' ideal, deception framing, and policing of female grooming under the guise of common sense.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Shreya. Age 26. Recently married. A woman working as a journalist in Delhi. You are writing an anonymous first person essay for an online publication about the experience of being asked at your husband's family's first large gathering after the wedding why you are not wearing sindoor. The essay is about the specific social mechanics of that moment Ñ who asked, how it was asked, what the room felt like, what your husband did, what you said, what you wished you had said, and what it revealed about the invisible contract you had entered when you got married that nobody had shown you in writing.
